ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class; no specific grapefruit-infused green tea entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers liquid green tea; no specific grapefruit-flavoured variant exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for grapefruit green tea beverage identified; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a typical takeaway cup of grapefruit green tea sold at Singaporean bubble tea or specialty tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Grapefruit Green Tea (No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Item D08010033
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record D08010033 for Grapefruit Green Tea (No Topping). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this item; set to 0.0 as this is a low-fat beverage with no dairy or added fat components. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and detailed micronutrients not reported by HPB for this item; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — sugar-sweetened fruit tea / sweetened green tea analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grapefruit green tea as sold in Singapore. GI estimated at 52 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened green tea / fruit-juice-sweetened tea beverages with comparable sugar content (~7–8 g/100 ml). Grapefruit juice itself has a GI of approximately 48 (University of Sydney GI Database). The blended beverage with added sugar is estimated at GI 52 (low range). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (52 × 7.49 × 3.5) / 100 = 13.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.49 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; grapefruit compounds (furanocoumarins) may interact with antihypertensive medications including calcium channel blockers
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request an unsweetened version of this beverage and consult their prescriber before consuming grapefruit-containing products if taking antihypertensive medications such as amlodipine or felodipine, as grapefruit furanocoumarins inhibit CYP3A4 and can raise drug plasma levels.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.49 g sugar per 100 g and 0 g dietary fibre; liquid sugar without fibre causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; a 350 ml serving delivers approximately 26 g of free sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; an unsweetened version using no-calorie sweetener or plain grapefruit green tea without added sugar is strongly preferred to prevent postprandial hyperglycaemia, and grapefruit may interact with certain diabetes medications — consult a pharmacist.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (7.49 g/100 g) in a liquid form contributes to excess caloric intake, hypertriglyceridaemia, and cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly; green tea catechins (EGCG) and grapefruit flavonoids provide partial cardioprotective offset
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, regular consumption of this sweetened beverage is not recommended due to its free-sugar load; an unsweetened preparation retains the cardioprotective polyphenols from green tea and grapefruit without the cardiovascular risk associated with sugar-sweetened beverages (AHA 2018 dietary guidelines).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Green tea catechins (EGCG) and vitamin C from grapefruit juice provide anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity; added sugar may mildly promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This beverage may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis for its anti-inflammatory polyphenol content, but an unsweetened preparation is preferred to minimise pro-inflammatory effects of free sugars; grapefruit may interact with RA medications — consult prescriber.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grapefruit Green Tea (No Topping) is a freshly prepared cold or chilled specialty tea beverage popular in Singapore, made by infusing brewed green tea with grapefruit juice and typically sweetened with sugar or a sugar syrup. The drink is served without any additional toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding, making it a lighter option within the specialty tea category. The flavour profile combines the mild astringency and grassy notes of green tea with the tart, citrusy brightness of grapefruit, resulting in a refreshing beverage with a slightly sweet-sour balance. Per 100 g, it provides 29 kcal, 7.49 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0.01 g protein, 0 g fat, and 1 mg sodium, classifying it as a low-calorie, low-sodium, sugar-sweetened beverage. The beverage contains no dietary fibre, meaning its sugar content is rapidly absorbed. Green tea contributes catechins including epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), which have documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Grapefruit juice contributes vitamin C and flavonoids including naringenin, which have mild cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory effects. However, grapefruit furanocoumarins inhibit CYP3A4 and can interact with numerous medications including antihypertensives, statins, and immunosuppressants. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose, unless prepared without added sugar. It is sold widely at Singaporean bubble tea chains and specialty tea stalls.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record D08010033, which is the canonical approved primary source for Singapore food items. All core macros are present and consistent with the food type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo record D08010033; this is a water-based tea and juice beverage containing no added fat, dairy, or oil — fat is naturally zero by formulation. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with zero total fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB; grapefruit juice without pulp contributes no measurable fibre in this preparation. protein_g is 0.01 g/100 g (trace level) as reported by HPB — not zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this item (D08010033) as of 2026. Grapefruit furanocoumarins are a known CYP3A4 inhibitor — this beverage carries a drug-interaction caution relevant to clinical settings. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened green tea / fruit-juice-sweetened tea entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product has been published. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (52 × 7.49 × 3.5) / 100 = 13.6 g.
